label: Principle of relativity
body: The laws of physics are the same in all \cref{Inertial frames}{2.1}. No experiment can distinguish uniform motion from rest.   When combined with the \cref{Constancy of the speed of light}{1.2}, this postulate determines the form of the \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}, which are the cornerstone of special relativity.

label: Constancy of the speed of light
body: Light in vacuum propagates at the same speed \(c\) for all \cref{Inertial frames}{2.1}.   Combined with the \cref{Principle of relativity}{1.1}, this eliminates absolute simultaneity and implies that transformations between frames must preserve a single invariant measure — the \cref{Spacetime interval}{3.2}.   That invariant structure is expressed mathematically through the \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1} and later encoded by the \cref{Metric tensor}{3.3}.

label: Inertial frames
body: Reference frames in which a free particle moves at constant velocity unless acted upon by forces.   According to the \cref{Principle of relativity}{1.1}, the laws of physics take the same form in all inertial frames.    Coordinates describing a \cref{Spacetime event}{2.2} in different inertial frames are related by the \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}.   These transformations preserve the invariant geometry encoded in the \cref{Spacetime interval}{3.2}.

label: Spacetime event
body: A point in spacetime representing where and when a physical occurrence takes place.   In a given \cref{Inertial frame}{2.1}, an event is described by four coordinates  \[ (ct,x,y,z) \]  which together form the \cref{Position four-vector}{4.3}.    The separation between two events is characterized by the \cref{Spacetime interval}{3.2}, which remains invariant under \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}.

label: Principle of locality
body: Physical interactions depend only on quantities defined at the same \cref{Spacetime event}{2.2}.   Fields and particles influence one another through local interactions rather than instantaneous action at a distance.  Combined with the \cref{Constancy of the speed of light}{1.2}, locality implies that causal influence propagates within the \cref{Light cone}{3.4} structure of spacetime.

label: Lorentz transformations
body: Linear transformations connecting coordinates of a \cref{Spacetime event}{2.2} between different \cref{Inertial frames}{2.1}.    They preserve the invariant quantity known as the \cref{Spacetime interval}{3.2}, ensuring consistency with both the \cref{Principle of relativity}{1.1} and the \cref{Constancy of the speed of light}{1.2}.    In matrix form the transformation acts on a \cref{Four-vector}{4.2}, leaving invariant inner products defined using the \cref{Metric tensor}{3.3}.

label: Spacetime interval
body:
The invariant measure of separation between two \cref{Spacetime events}{2.2}.   Using coordinates in any \cref{Inertial frame}{2.1}, it is defined as  \[ s^2 = \eta_{\mu
u} x^\mu x^
u    = c^2 t^2 - x^2 - y^2 - z^2 \]  where \( \eta_{\mu
u} \) is the \cref{Metric tensor}{3.3}.    This quantity remains unchanged under the \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}, making it the fundamental invariant of Minkowski spacetime.

label: Metric tensor
body: The tensor  \[ \eta_{\mu\nu} = \mathrm{diag}(1,-1,-1,-1) \]  defines inner products of \cref{Four-vectors}{4.2} and determines the \cref{Spacetime interval}{3.2}.    It encodes the geometric structure of Minkowski spacetime and remains invariant under \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}.

label: Light cone
body: The set of spacetime directions for which the \cref{Spacetime interval}{3.2} satisfies  \[ s^2 = 0 \]  corresponding to the path followed by light signals.    The light cone divides spacetime into causal past, causal future, and spacelike regions, ensuring compatibility with the \cref{Principle of locality}{2.3}.

label: Minkowski diagram
body: A geometric representation of spacetime in which \cref{Spacetime events}{2.2} are plotted using time and space axes.    The \cref{Light cone}{3.4} appears as lines at \(45^\circ\), illustrating the invariant causal structure of spacetime.   The effect of \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1} appears as a hyperbolic rotation of the coordinate axes.

label: Proper time
body: The invariant time measured along the world-line of a particle moving between two \cref{Spacetime events}{2.2}.   For motion in a given \cref{Inertial frame}{2.1}, the infinitesimal interval of proper time is defined by the \cref{Spacetime interval}{3.2}:  \[ d\tau^2 = dt^2 - \frac{dx^2 + dy^2 + dz^2}{c^2}. \]  Unlike coordinate time, proper time is the same for all observers related by \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}.   It therefore provides a natural parameter for relativistic motion.

label: Four-vectors
body: Objects with four components that transform linearly under \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}.   A four-vector \(A^\mu\) has components  \[ A^\mu = (A^0,A^1,A^2,A^3) \]  whose invariant inner product is defined using the \cref{Metric tensor}{3.3}.    Examples include the \cref{Position four-vector}{4.3}, the \cref{Velocity four-vector}{4.4}, and the \cref{Momentum four-vector}{4.5}.   Four-vectors provide a compact way of expressing relativistic laws so that their invariance under changes of \cref{Inertial frames}{2.1} is manifest.

label: Position four-vector
body: The coordinates of a \cref{Spacetime event}{2.2} arranged into a \cref{Four-vector}{4.2}:  \[ x^\mu = (ct,x,y,z). \]  Differences between position four-vectors determine the \cref{Spacetime interval}{3.2}.   Under \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}, the components of \(x^\mu\) mix so that different observers assign different space and time coordinates to the same event while preserving the invariant interval.

label: Velocity four-vector
body: Defined as the derivative of the \cref{Position four-vector}{4.3} with respect to \cref{Proper time}{4.1}:  \[ u^\mu = \frac{dx^\mu}{d\tau}. \]  Because proper time is invariant, the velocity four-vector transforms as a true \cref{Four-vector}{4.2}.   Its invariant magnitude satisfies  \[ u^\mu u_\mu = c^2. \]  Multiplying the velocity four-vector by mass yields the \cref{Momentum four-vector}{4.5}.

label: Momentum four-vector
body: The relativistic generalization of momentum, defined by  \[ p^\mu = m u^\mu, \]  where \(u^\mu\) is the \cref{Velocity four-vector}{4.4}.    Its components are  \[ p^\mu = (E/c,\,\vec p), \]  combining energy and three-momentum into a single \cref{Four-vector}{4.2}.   The invariant magnitude of \(p^\mu\) expresses the relativistic energy–momentum relation.

label: Action principle
body: The dynamics of a physical system are determined by the requirement that the action  \[ S = \int L\, dt \]  is stationary with respect to small variations of the path.    Here \(L\) is the \cref{Lagrangian}{5.2}.   This principle provides a unified way of deriving equations of motion consistent with relativistic invariance under \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}.

label: Lagrangian
body: A function of generalized coordinates and velocities whose integral defines the action in the \cref{Action principle}{5.1}.    For relativistic systems the Lagrangian can be constructed using invariant quantities derived from the \cref{Spacetime interval}{3.2} or from combinations of \cref{Four-vectors}{4.2}.    The equations of motion follow from the \cref{Euler–Lagrange equations}{5.3}.

label: Euler–Lagrange equations
body: The equations obtained by varying the action defined in the \cref{Action principle}{5.1}.   For a coordinate \(q\) they take the form  \[ \frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot q} - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q} = 0. \]  These equations determine the dynamics implied by a chosen \cref{Lagrangian}{5.2}.

label: Hamiltonian formalism
body: A reformulation of mechanics derived from the \cref{Lagrangian}{5.2} by introducing the \cref{Canonical momentum}{5.5}.   The Hamiltonian is defined by  \[ H = p\dot q - L. \]  Time evolution is then described by Hamilton’s equations.   Although equivalent to the \cref{Euler–Lagrange equations}{5.3}, this formulation clarifies the structure of conserved quantities.

label: Canonical momentum
body: The momentum conjugate to a coordinate \(q\), defined from the \cref{Lagrangian}{5.2} as  \[ p = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot q}. \]  In relativistic systems it is closely related to the \cref{Momentum four-vector}{4.5}.   Canonical momentum plays a central role in the \cref{Hamiltonian formalism}{5.4} and later in the introduction of electromagnetic interaction.

label: Noether's theorem
body: A fundamental result connecting symmetry and conservation laws.   Every continuous symmetry of the action in the \cref{Action principle}{5.1} implies a conserved quantity.  For example, invariance under spacetime translations leads to conservation of energy and momentum, which are later expressed through the \cref{Energy–momentum tensor}{9.4}.

label: Scalar field
body: A quantity that assigns a single value to every \cref{Spacetime event}{2.2}.   A scalar field is therefore a function  \[ \phi(x) \]  defined over spacetime.    Unlike a \cref{Four-vector}{4.2}, a scalar field has the same value for all observers related by \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}.   Its dynamics are determined by a \cref{Field Lagrangian}{6.3}, whose variation using the \cref{Euler–Lagrange equations}{5.3} produces relativistic \cref{Field equations}{6.4}.

label: Vector field
body: A field whose components transform as a \cref{Four-vector}{4.2} under \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}.   At each \cref{Spacetime event}{2.2} it assigns four quantities  \[ A_\mu(x). \]  Vector fields generalize the idea of a relativistic particle variable to systems with infinitely many degrees of freedom.   Their dynamics are determined by a \cref{Field Lagrangian}{6.3}, extending the \cref{Lagrangian}{5.2} formalism to fields.

label: Field Lagrangian
body: A local density  \[ \mathcal{L}(\phi,\partial_\mu \phi) \]  defined at every \cref{Spacetime event}{2.2}.   The action for a field becomes  \[ S = \int \mathcal{L}\, d^4x, \]  extending the \cref{Action principle}{5.1} from particles to continuous fields.    Applying the \cref{Euler–Lagrange equations}{5.3} to this action produces the corresponding \cref{Field equations}{6.4}.   Local dependence on the field and its derivatives reflects the \cref{Principle of locality}{2.3}.

label: Field equations
body: Differential equations governing the evolution of a field.   They arise from the \cref{Euler–Lagrange equations}{5.3} applied to a \cref{Field Lagrangian}{6.3}.    Because the Lagrangian density is constructed from quantities transforming covariantly under \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}, the resulting field equations respect relativistic invariance.   For a \cref{Scalar field}{6.1} these equations reduce to the relativistic wave equation, while for a \cref{Vector field}{6.2} they describe electromagnetic dynamics.

label: Vector potential \(A_\mu\)
body: A \cref{Vector field}{6.2} defined at every \cref{Spacetime event}{2.2}.   Rather than describing the electric and magnetic fields directly, electromagnetism is formulated in terms of this potential field.    The observable electromagnetic quantities arise from derivatives of \(A_\mu\), forming the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}.   The equations governing \(A_\mu\) follow from an electromagnetic \cref{Field Lagrangian}{6.3}.

label: Field tensor \(F_{\mu\nu}\)
body:
An antisymmetric tensor constructed from derivatives of the \cref{Vector potential}{7.1}:  \[ F_{\mu
u} = \partial_\mu A_
u - \partial_
u A_\mu. \]  This tensor transforms covariantly under \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}.   Its components contain the physical \cref{Electric field}{7.3} and \cref{Magnetic field}{7.4}.   In relativistic form, the entire \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5} is encoded in \(F_{\mu
u}\).

label: Electric field
body: One component of the relativistic \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}.   In terms of the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}, the electric field components are  \[ E_i = F_{0i}. \]  The electric field describes the force acting on charges at rest in a given \cref{Inertial frame}{2.1}.   Under \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}, electric and magnetic fields mix with one another.

label: Magnetic field
body: The second component of the relativistic \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}.   Its components arise from the spatial parts of the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}.    Magnetic fields act on moving charges and currents.   In special relativity the magnetic field is not an independent entity but rather part of the same geometric structure as the \cref{Electric field}{7.3}.

label: Electromagnetic field
body: The unified relativistic field combining the \cref{Electric field}{7.3} and \cref{Magnetic field}{7.4}.   This field is fully represented by the antisymmetric \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}.    The dynamics of the electromagnetic field are governed by \cref{Maxwell's equations}{7.6}, which follow from an electromagnetic \cref{Field Lagrangian}{6.3}.

label: Maxwell's equations
body: The relativistic field equations governing the \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}.   In compact covariant form they may be written  \[ \partial_\mu F^{\mu\nu} = J^\nu. \]  These equations describe how electric charges and currents act as sources for the electromagnetic field.   They arise from the \cref{Euler–Lagrange equations}{5.3} applied to the electromagnetic \cref{Field Lagrangian}{6.3}.

label: Lorenz gauge
body: A condition imposed on the \cref{Vector potential}{7.1}:  \[ \partial_\mu A^\mu = 0. \]  This gauge choice simplifies \cref{Maxwell's equations}{7.6}, allowing them to be written as wave equations for the potential.   The physical \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5} remains unchanged under such gauge transformations.

label: Gauge invariance
body: A fundamental symmetry of the electromagnetic theory in which the \cref{Vector potential}{7.1} can be transformed according to  \[ A_\mu \rightarrow A_\mu + \partial_\mu \Lambda \]  without changing the physical \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}. Because the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2} depends only on derivatives of the potential, it remains unchanged under such transformations.  Gauge invariance therefore reflects a redundancy in the description of the electromagnetic field.  The freedom to choose different potentials describing the same physics leads naturally to conditions such as the \cref{Lorenz gauge}{7.7}.

label: Four-current
body: The relativistic quantity describing the distribution and motion of electric charge.  It forms a \cref{Four-vector}{4.2} with components  \[ J^\mu = (c\rho,\vec J), \]  where \( \rho \) is charge density and \( \vec J \) is current density.  The four-current acts as the source term in \cref{Maxwell's equations}{7.6}. Its conservation is expressed through the continuity equation discussed under \cref{Charge conservation}{8.5}.

label: Minimal coupling
body: The prescription for introducing electromagnetic interaction into the \cref{Lagrangian}{5.2} of a charged particle.  The ordinary momentum is replaced by  \[ p_\mu \rightarrow p_\mu - eA_\mu, \]  where \(A_\mu\) is the \cref{Vector potential}{7.1}.   This modification preserves \cref{Gauge invariance}{8.1} and leads directly to the dynamical law governing charged particles in electromagnetic fields.

label: Lorentz force law
body:
The relativistic equation of motion for a charged particle interacting with the \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}.  In covariant form it can be written  \[  rac{dp^\mu}{d	au} = eF^{\mu
u}u_
u, \]  where \(p^\mu\) is the \cref{Momentum four-vector}{4.5}, \(u^\mu\) is the \cref{Velocity four-vector}{4.4}, and \(F^{\mu
u}\) is the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}.  This equation arises naturally from applying the \cref{Action principle}{5.1} to a charged particle interacting with the electromagnetic field via \cref{Minimal coupling}{8.3}.

label: Charge conservation
body: Electric charge is locally conserved.  In relativistic form this statement is expressed by the continuity equation  \[ \partial_\mu J^\mu = 0, \]  where \(J^\mu\) is the \cref{Four-current}{8.2}.   This relation ensures the internal consistency of \cref{Maxwell's equations}{7.6} and reflects the symmetry structure of the electromagnetic theory.

label: Energy density of EM field
body: The electromagnetic field itself carries energy.  The energy density stored in the \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5} is  \[ u = \frac12 (E^2 + B^2), \]  where \(E\) and \(B\) denote the \cref{Electric field}{7.3} and \cref{Magnetic field}{7.4}.   This energy contributes to the relativistic conservation laws described by the \cref{Energy--momentum tensor}{9.4}.

label: Momentum density (Poynting vector)
body: Electromagnetic fields also carry momentum.   The momentum density is given by the Poynting vector  \[ \vec S = \vec E \times \vec B. \]  This vector represents the flow of energy and momentum carried by the \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}.   Together with the \cref{Energy density of EM field}{9.1}, it forms part of the relativistic description of field energy and momentum.

label: Stress–energy of EM field
body: The electromagnetic field exerts stresses and transports energy and momentum through spacetime.   These properties are summarized by the electromagnetic stress–energy tensor, constructed from the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2} and the \cref{Metric tensor}{3.3}.  This tensor describes how electromagnetic energy and momentum propagate and interact with matter.

label: Energy--momentum tensor
body: A rank-two tensor \(T^{\mu\nu}\) describing the density and flow of energy and momentum in spacetime.  For the electromagnetic field it is constructed from the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}.  Conservation of energy and momentum is expressed by  \[ \partial_\mu T^{\mu\nu} = 0. \]  This conservation law follows from spacetime translation symmetry via \cref{Noether's theorem}{5.6}.

label: Electromagnetic waves
body: Self-propagating oscillations of the \cref{Electric field}{7.3} and \cref{Magnetic field}{7.4}.   In regions where the \cref{Four-current}{8.2} vanishes, the field equations for the \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5} reduce to wave equations, allowing disturbances in the field to propagate through space.  These waves travel at the speed \(c\), consistent with the \cref{Constancy of the speed of light}{1.2}.   Electromagnetic waves carry energy and momentum, described by the \cref{Energy--momentum tensor}{9.4} and transported through space according to the \cref{Momentum density (Poynting vector)}{9.2}.

label: Wave equation
body: In the \cref{Lorenz gauge}{7.7}, the equations governing the \cref{Vector potential}{7.1} take the form  \[ \square A_\mu = J_\mu, \]  where \(J_\mu\) is the \cref{Four-current}{8.2} and \(\square = \partial_\mu \partial^\mu\) is the d'Alembertian operator.  When the source term vanishes, the potential satisfies a free wave equation. Solutions correspond to \cref{Electromagnetic waves}{10.1} propagating at the speed determined by the spacetime structure encoded in the \cref{Metric tensor}{3.3}.

label: Radiation reaction
body: When charged particles accelerate, they emit \cref{Electromagnetic waves}{10.1}.   Because these waves carry energy and momentum described by the \cref{Energy--momentum tensor}{9.4}, the emitting particle must experience a recoil force.  This phenomenon modifies the motion predicted by the \cref{Lorentz force law}{8.4} and represents the back–reaction of the field on its source.

label: Lorentz invariance
body: A fundamental symmetry stating that the laws of physics have the same form in all \cref{Inertial frames}{2.1}.   Mathematically, this means the equations governing physical quantities are covariant under \cref{Lorentz transformations}{3.1}.  Objects such as \cref{Four-vectors}{4.2}, the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2}, and the \cref{Energy--momentum tensor}{9.4} transform in ways that preserve the invariant structure defined by the \cref{Metric tensor}{3.3}.   Lorentz invariance is therefore a guiding principle in constructing relativistic physical theories.

label: Gauge fixing
body: Because of \cref{Gauge invariance}{8.1}, many different \cref{Vector potentials}{7.1} correspond to the same physical \cref{Electromagnetic field}{7.5}.   Gauge fixing is the procedure of choosing a particular representative from this equivalence class.  An example is the \cref{Lorenz gauge}{7.7}, which simplifies the equations governing the potential by turning \cref{Maxwell's equations}{7.6} into wave equations.   Although different gauge choices alter the mathematical form of the potential, observable quantities derived from the \cref{Field tensor}{7.2} remain unchanged.
